texts.label: text
texts.orig: The energy penalty is a primary limitation for the implementation of the aqueous solvents for large-scale postcombustion CO2 capture processes. In this study, a novel aqueous-based phase change solvent, composed of potassium glycinate (GlyK, reactive species), water (H2O, solvent) and dimethylformamide (DMF, antisolvent) was developed to improve the energy efficiency of CO2 capture. To examine the role of the antisolvent, a series of aqueous-based amino acid solvents (GlyK-X) with different DMF:H2O (X) volume ratios was prepared, fully characterized and assessed. It was observed that a CO2-free phase appeared at the top of the aqueous-based amino acid GlyK-X solvents after CO2 absorption which can be easily separated and recycled to the absorption column and save energy. The results showed that the GlyK-60 solvent with DMF:H2O volume ratio of 60:40 had a very high CO2-free phase volume (63%). texts.label: text
texts.orig: Over the past few decades, increasing greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions and global temperature increases has become of concern [1,2]. Anthropogenic carbon dioxide (CO2) emission, primarily from fossil fuel combustion (e.g., coal, oil and natural gas) for power generation, is the second most abundant GHG after water vapor and the main cause of global temperature rise in the atmosphere [3,4]. Despite international protocols (e.g., Kyoto Protocol) and agreements (e.g., Paris Agreement), the atmospheric CO2 concentration has increased dramatically from the beginning of the industrial revolution (280 ppm in 1750) to date (412 ppm in 2019) resulting in an 0.8 °C elevation in global temperature [5].

texts.orig: Aqueous solutions of amino acid salts, containing the same functional groups as alkanolamines, has been identified as a promising alternative for amine solutions due to their physical-chemical properties including negligible volatility, low toxicity and high resistance to oxygen degradation [18,19]. In recent years, several sodium or potassium amino acid solutions, including glycine [20,21], proline [21-23], arginine [20,21,24], lysine [21,25], histidine [21,26] and sarcosine [20,21], have been reported as potential candidates for CO2 removal. However, the regeneration of aqueous amino acid solutions is still energy-intensive and poses serious barriers to worldwide large-scale implementation.

texts.orig: While many investigations have been performed to develop novel energy-efficient solvents for commercial applications, phase change solvents have received increasing interest recently as another alternative solvent approach [19,27,28]. The replacement of water with organic solutions in non-aqueous phase change solvents can lead to two separate CO2-lean and CO2-rich phases after CO2 absorption. Hence, only the regeneration of the lower CO2-rich phase is required while the upper CO2-lean phase can be directly recycled to the absorption column. This significantly decreases the required energy during the regeneration process. Zheng et al. [29] studied the phase change behavior of a new non-aqueous solvent formed by the dissolution of triethylenetetramine (TETA) in ethanol (TETA/ethanol) for CO2 removal. The authors observed that solid TETA-carbamate precipitants formed during CO2 absorption in the TETA/ethanol solvent, whereas there was no precipitation in the TETA/water solvent. In a similar work, Cheng et al. [30] dissolved N -methyl-1,3-diaminopropane (MAPA) in N , N -dimethylformamide (DMF) and reported that solid precipitates were generated after CO2 capture. texts.label: text
texts.orig: As proline is the only amino acid which can be effectively dissolved in non-aqueous solutions [31], the development of amino acid phase change solvents has been limited. Recently, a handful of research works [32,33] found that amino acids can be dissolved in aqueous-based solutions and confirmed the phase change feature of some amino acid solvents. However, the aqueous-based phase change amino acid solvent is a recently-emerged concept and further detailed investigations are still required for worldwide acceptance of this energy-efficient technology. Here, for the first time, the CO2 absorption/desorption of aqueous-based phase change amino acid solvents as a more conducive replacement for non-aqueous phase change amine solvents was examined. The potassium glycinate was used as the amino acid, due to its similar structure to MEA and high kinetics of CO2 absorption, for the investigation of aqueous-based amino acid solvents. DMF was chosen as the physical antisolvent (instead of typical alcohols, glycols and ionic liquids) due to its benefits such as low vapor pressure and volatility, high boiling point, low viscosity and comparable price for large-scale implementation. The equilibrium and dynamic CO2 absorption capacity, phase separation and species distribution were also studied. The regeneration and heat duty of aqueous-based amino acid solvents were also determined and compared to aqueous potassium glycinate and MEA solvent as baselines.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To prepare potassium glycinate (GlyK) solution, 112.61 g (1.5 mol) of Gly and 84.15 g (1.5 mol) of KOH were thoroughly mixed and dissolved in the 500 mL aqueous/aqueous-based solutions. As a result, the molar concentration of GlyK was kept constant at 3 mol/L (M) in all prepared solutions. Pure water was used as the aqueous solvent and aqueous-based solvents were prepared with different DMF:H2O volume ratios of 10:90 (GlyK-10), 25:75 (GlyK-25), 40:60 (GlyK-40), 50:50 (GlyK-50), 55:45 (GlyK-55), 60:40 (GlyK-60) and 65:35 (GlyK-65).

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.3. CO2 absorption/desorption experiments
texts.text: 2.3. CO2 absorption/desorption experiments

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Dynamic CO2 absorption experiments were conducted in an OptiMax TM workstation 1001 (Mettler-Toledo) equipped with a 1000 mL reactor, a highly accurate temperature controller and an adjustable mixing system covered by a jacketed vessel for stringent heat transfer (Fig. 1a). In a typical experiment, 500 mL solvent was introduced into the reactor and simulated post-combustion flue gas at a constant flow rate of 635 mL/min (15 vol% CO2 and 85 vol% N2) was bubbled into the solvent at 40 °C and approximately atmospheric pressure (40 kPa(g)) under 400 rpm rotation speed. A sponge sparger was used to create bubbles to enhance gas distribution and avoid line blockage by solid precipitates. Two separate gas flow controllers were employed to provide constant CO2 (Aalborg, CO2-GFC17, 0-100 mL/ min) and N2 (Aalborg, N2-GFC17, 0-10 L/min) flow rates. The treated gas stream was cooled by a condenser located at the top of the reactor to recycle the evaporated solvent into the reactor. Any remaining solvent in the gas stream was condensed by two consecutive cold traps and passed through an online CO2 analyzer (BlueSens, BCP-CO2) to dynamically record CO2 concentration at an interval of 2 sec. The calibrated gas flowmeter (Aalborg, GFM17, 0-1000 mL/min) was utilized to accurately measure the volumetric flow rate of the treated gas stream. After the absorption experiment, the status of the OptiMax TM workstation changed from absorption to desorption. For phase change solvents (i.e., GlyK-50, GlyK-55, GlyK-60 and GlyK-65), the upper liquid phase was removed by an external vacuum pump and only lower liquid phase and solid precipitates remaining in the reactor for desorption. In desorption experiments, the reactor temperature was increased from 40 °C to the boiling temperature of the solvent (100-110 °C) and was maintained at this temperature for the entire regeneration time (130 min for GlyK-0 to GlyK-40 solvents without phase change feature and 80 min for phase change GlyK-50 to GlyK-65 solvents). Pure N2 flow was continuously bubbled into the solution as a carrier gas with a volumetric flow rate of 540 mL/min. The temperature of the condenser was maintained at 3-5 °C to recycle evaporated solvent into the reactor.

texts.orig: 2.4. Solubility measurement of CO2
texts.text: 2.4. Solubility measurement of CO2

texts.label: text
texts.orig: An inhouse rig was utilized to measure the CO2 solubility (or equilibrium CO2 absorption capacity) in the aqueous and aqueousbased amino acid solvents. The schematic diagram of CO2 solubility rig is presented in Fig. 1b. All absorption experiments were carried out in the pressure range of 0-150 kPa(g) at 40 °C to simulate the flue gas conditions. Prior to each experiment, the system was evacuated by an external vacuum pump to remove any remaining gas molecules and purged by a pure N2 stream. For each CO2 absorption experiment, a 100 mL sample was weighed and loaded in a stainless-steel reactor with a volume of 325 mL, thoroughly sealed and placed into a water bath until the temperature of the reactor reached the set-point temperature (40 °C) and the reactor pressure remained stable. Then, the reactor was connected to the CO2 container with a 145 mL volume and the desired amount of pure CO2 was injected into the equilibrium reactor in batch steps. The equilibrium condition was achieved when the pressure of the reactor maintained constant. The temperature of water bath was adjusted and maintained at 40 °C by a water circulator (Ratek, TH6

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The phase separation behavior of different aqueous-based GlyK-X solvents at the end of CO2 absorption process is provided in Fig. 2. As seen, GlyK-10 solvent showed no phase change behavior and remained completely homogeneous upon CO2 absorption. By increasing the DMF:H2O ratio from GlyK-10, the system started precipitating in the form of white solid particles. Interestingly, Fig. 2 illustrates that the liquid-liquid-solid separation occurred in the GlyK-40 solvent with a nearly invisible interface layer. The liquid-liquid-solid phase change behavior was first observed in GlyK-50 solvent with a blurred liquidliquid interface and remained the main phase change mechanism until GlyK-65.
texts.text: The phase separation behavior of different aqueous-based GlyK-X solvents at the end of CO2 absorption process is provided in Fig. 2. As seen, GlyK-10 solvent showed no phase change behavior and remained completely homogeneous upon CO2 absorption. By increasing the DMF:H2O ratio from GlyK-10, the system started precipitating in the form of white solid particles. Interestingly, Fig. 2 illustrates that the liquid-liquid-solid separation occurred in the GlyK-40 solvent with a nearly invisible interface layer. The liquid-liquid-solid phase change behavior was first observed in GlyK-50 solvent with a blurred liquidliquid interface and remained the main phase change mechanism until GlyK-65.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To quantify the phase separation of aqueous-based GlyK-X solvents, the solid precipitates were recovered by vacuum filtration and the two remaining liquid phases were separated by a separation funnel. The volume of liquid and solid phases was accurately measured using a graduated cylinder without any additional drying. The volume of different phases in GlyK-X solvents is reported in Fig. 3a. By increasing the DMF:H2O ratio from GlyK-40 to GlyK-60 the amount of precipitated solid particles increased from 25 (5% of total solvent) to 135 mL (27% of total solvent). In a similar trend, the volume of upper liquid phase increased from 150 mL (30% of total solvent) in GlyK-40 to 315 mL (63% of total solvent) in GlyK-60. As Wang and his colleagues [41,42] previously mentioned, the increased amount of upper liquid phase can be a promising signal in phase change solvents. The upper liquid phase is typically the CO2-lean phase and can be directly recycled to the absorption column without any thermal regeneration. Thus, increasing the volume of upper liquid phase can be interpreted as a reduction in the required energy for regeneration. On the other hand, by increasing the DMF:H2O ratio, the volume of lower liquid phase considerably diminished from 325 mL (65% of total solvent) in GlyK-40 to 50 mL (10% of total solvent) in GlyK-60. It was vividly observed that DMF properly played the role of antisolvent in aqueous-based GlyK-X solvents, decreasing the solubility of CO2-containing products and resulting in phase change behavior. Interestingly, the volume of different phases in GlyK-60 was nearly the same as that of GlyK-65 which clearly confirms the inefficiency of DMF in DMF:H2O volume ratios higher than 60:40. Although nearly all published research papers have proposed nonaqueous solutions for the preparation of phase change amine solvents [41-44], it should be pointed out here that this strategy is almost infeasible for amino acids. This is because of the limited solubility of amino acid salts in non-aqueous solvents while most of the amines can easily be dissolved in a large number of non-aqueous solutions without

texts.orig: To monitor the rate and quantity of solid precipitation, the FBRM probe was used during CO2 absorption. The dynamic solid precipitation behavior of different GlyK-X solvents is illustrated in Fig. 3b. For the GlyK-50 solvent, the precipitation began after 3.5 h (i.e., induction time), took 3 h to reach its maximum value (i.e., precipitation time) with a constant rate and stabilized after 6.5 h. As expected, increasing the concentration of DMF from GlyK-50 to GlyK-55 remarkably decreased the induction time of precipitation from 3.5 h to less than 1 h. However, the precipitation time remained 3.5 h in GlyK-55 compared to that of GlyK-50. Although increasing DMF:H2O ratio from GlyK-55 to GlyK-65 exhibited no considerable change in the induction time of precipitation, the precipitation time markedly dropped from 3 h in GlyK-55 to about 30 min in both GlyK-60 and GlyK-65 solvents.

texts.orig: 3.2. CO2 uptake of aqueous and aqueous-based GlyK-X solvents
texts.text: 3.2. CO2 uptake of aqueous and aqueous-based GlyK-X solvents

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The dynamic CO2 loading of aqueous and aqueous-based GlyK-X solvents at 40 °C and 40 kPa(g) are demonstrated in Fig. 4a. As it can be seen, the aqueous-based GlyK-10 solvent displayed nearly the same CO2 absorption capacity compared to the conventional aqueous GlyK-0 solvent. However, the additional amount of DMF in GlyK-25 solvent increased the CO2 absorption capacity during the first hour by 11.0% (0.446-0.495 mol/L) compared to GlyK-0. A simple comparison between the aqueous GlyK-0 and GlyK-25 solvent confirmed that the presence of DMF can enhance the kinetics of CO2 absorption, since GlyK-25 exhibited 11.0% (0.446-0.495 mol/L) and 8.55% (1.251-1.358 mol/L) higher CO2 absorption capacity during the first 1 and 3 h, respectively. This increment can be ascribed to the high solubility of CO2 in DMF compared to the water [30]. This property of DMF can decrease mass transfer resistance in the liquid phase, increase CO2 concentration in the aqueous-based solvent and promote the CO2-GlyK reaction. On the other hand, using the additional amount of DMF from GlyK-25 to GlyK-40 dropped the CO2 uptake by 13.73% (0.495-0.427 mol/L) and 8.76% (1.358-1.239 mol/L) during the first 1 and 3 h, respectively, and remained nearly constant until GlyK-65. This sudden decrease in the CO2 uptake may be related to the solid precipitation as the precipitation during CO2 absorption can remarkably increase the viscosity of the solvent, decrease mass transfer coefficients and adversely affect the kinetics of CO2 absorption.

By adding further DMF and starting solid precipitation, the equilibrium absorption capacity increased strikingly from 0.483 mol/mol in GlyK-40 and reached 0.546 mol/mol in GlyK-60. The positive effect of phase separation on the equilibrium absorption capacity may be attributed to the very low solubility of GlyK-carbamate (GlyK-COO - ), bicarbonate (HCO3 - ) and protonated GlyK (GlyK-H + ) in DMF/H2O solution. texts.orig: First, GlyK-COO - and GlyK-H + are formed as direct products of the GlyK-CO2 reaction. Afterward, the GlyK-COO - is partially hydrolyzed and forms HCO3 - and GlyK. Based on the first reaction (Scheme 2, Reaction (1)), the theoretical maximum absorption capacity is 0.5 mol CO2/mol GlyK (1:2 stoichiometry in which one molecule of CO2 needs two molecules of GlyK), while governing the second reaction (Scheme 2, Reaction (2)), can transfer CO2 from GlyK-COO to HCO3 - , recycle GlyK molecules (1:1 stoichiometry) and increase the equilibrium CO2 absorption capacity of the solvent.
texts.text: First, GlyK-COO - and GlyK-H + are formed as direct products of the GlyK-CO2 reaction. Afterward, the GlyK-COO - is partially hydrolyzed and forms HCO3 - and GlyK. Based on the first reaction (Scheme 2, Reaction (1)), the theoretical maximum absorption capacity is 0.5 mol CO2/mol GlyK (1:2 stoichiometry in which one molecule of CO2 needs two molecules of GlyK), while governing the second reaction (Scheme 2, Reaction (2)), can transfer CO2 from GlyK-COO to HCO3 - , recycle GlyK molecules (1:1 stoichiometry) and increase the equilibrium CO2 absorption capacity of the solvent.

texts.orig: Due to the low solubility of GlyK-COO - and GlyK-H + in the DMF/ H2O mixture, as the main cause of phase separation in the aqueousbased GlyK-40 to GlyK-65 solvents, these species are mainly accumulated in only one phase at a high concentration. The very high concentration of GlyK-COO - can favorably shift the carbamate hydrolysis reaction (Scheme 2, Reaction (2)) to the right side and promote HCO3 formation. Consequently, phase separation can inherently increase equilibrium CO2 absorption capacity of the aqueous-based GlyK-X solvents. This interpretation is consistent with the results of aqueous-base phase change GlyK-X solvents and can clearly explain why the equilibrium CO2 absorption capacity of GlyK-50 to GlyK-65 (0.513-0.551 mol/mol) was higher than the theoretical maximum CO2 absorption capacity of 0.5 mol/mol.

texts.orig: A comparison of the aqueous GlyK-0 and aqueous-based phase change GlyK-60 solvents revealed that though both of these solvents had nearly the same CO2 uptake during the first 1 and 3 h (Fig. 4a), GlyK-60 possessed 26.1% higher equilibrium CO2 absorption capacity than that of the conventional aqueous GlyK-0 solvent (Fig. 4b). In addition, the equilibrium absorption capacity remained approximately stable with increasing the DMF content from GlyK-60 (0.546 mol/mol) to GlyK-65 (0.551 mol/mol). Thus, the aqueous-based GlyK-60 solvent can be suggested as the best aqueous-based phase change amino acid solution among all GlyK solvents from CO2 absorption point of view.

texts.orig: The 1 H NMR and 13 C NMR of the upper liquid phase, lower liquid phase and solid precipitates belonging to the aqueous-based GlyK-60 (as the representative of aqueous-based phase change GlyK-X solvents) are depicted in Fig. 6. The results demonstrate that the upper liquid phase is a pure mixture of DMF/H2O without any impurity (e.g., GlyK/ GlyK-H + or other CO2-containing species). Huang et al. [45] reported that recycling the upper CO2-lean phase to the absorption column without any thermal regeneration can slightly decrease the cyclic absorption-desorption capacity due to the presence of remained CO2containing species in the solvent. Liu and his colleagues [46] similarly asserted that the presence of CO2-containing species is unavoidable in the upper liquid phase due to the low solubility of carbamate and bicarbonate. However, herein, it was observed that the upper liquid phase of GlyK-60 solvent is thoroughly free from CO2-containing species. To the best of the authors knowledge, this is the first reported phase change solvent with CO2-free upper liquid phase which not only can decrease the amount of energy consumption during regeneration but also can substantially improve the cyclic absorption-desorption capacity of GlyK-X solvents.
texts.text: The 1 H NMR and 13 C NMR of the upper liquid phase, lower liquid phase and solid precipitates belonging to the aqueous-based GlyK-60 (as the representative of aqueous-based phase change GlyK-X solvents) are depicted in Fig. 6. Liu and his colleagues [46] similarly asserted that the presence of CO2-containing species is unavoidable in the upper liquid phase due to the low solubility of carbamate and bicarbonate. texts.orig: The evaluation of 1 H NMR and 13 C NMR spectra disclosed that the white precipitated particles are only composed of GlyK/GlyK-H + species. Considering the high absorption capacity of aqueous-based GlyK-X solvents (see Fig. 4), the share of unreacted GlyK is small compared to the GlyK-H + as one of the main products of CO2-GlyK reaction. Therefore, the solid precipitants in the aqueous-based GlyK-X solvents are mainly crystalized GlyK-H + . This differs from other phase separation solvents previously reported by literature. Li et al. [47] and Cheng et al. [30] claimed that the precipitated solid products are mainly composed of carbamate and bicarbonate species in the non-aqueous amine/DMF solvents. In the same way, Shen and his colleagues [31,32] displayed that the bicarbonate and proline salts are the main species in the precipitates in the non-aqueous solvent of potassium proline/ ethanol. The main cause of the raised discrepancy only can be related to the synchronous role of aqueous and non-aqueous solutions during the absorptive reaction of amino acids with CO2 molecules. Both GlyK and GlyK-H + species have an extremely low solubility in DMF, while H2O can easily dissolve these species. Therefore, immediately after the formation of GlyK-H + , this species is accumulated in the aqueous phase causing milky/foggy condition in the solvent without any solid precipitation. Over time, which equals the induction time of precipitation (refer to Section 3.1), the concentration of GlyK-H + passes its solubility threshold and crystalized solid GlyK-H + species with high purity start to precipitate in the solvent. This threshold depends on the amount of antisolvent in aqueous-based GlyK-X solvents. Contrarily, protonated amines have a high solubility in the non-aqueous solvent and CO2containing products of amines are more prone to precipitate [48]. Due to the low solubility of produced GlyK-COO - and HCO3 - in DMF, these species remain in the water, concentrate and form a separate liquid phase (i.e., lower liquid phase). The original 1 H NMR and 13 C NMR spectra of all samples can be found in the Supplementary Information.

texts.orig: According to the results presented in Fig. 8a and b, the heat duty of aqueous MEA-30% and GlyK-0 solvent for this study were 16.04 and 15.47 kJ/kg-CO2, respectively. On the other hand, Zhang et al. [34,36] and Guo et al. [39] recorded 22.7 and 10.8 kJ/kg-CO2 as the required heat duty for the regeneration of MEA-30% solvent. This discrepancy may be attributed to the regeneration time, insulation and the efficiency of the instruments used in each study which affects the measured heat duty. Thus, the relative heat duty using the same equipment can be employed as a more reliable indicator of energy reduction.

texts.orig: Compared to the aqueous GlyK-0 solvent, the aqueous-based phase change solvents including GlyK-50 (575.3 kJ/kg-solvent and 7.52 kJ/ kg-CO2), GlyK-55 (541.3 kJ/kg-solvent and 7.08 kJ/kg-CO2), GlyK-60 (483.3 kJ/kg-solvent and 6.32 kJ/kg-CO2) and GlyK-65 (485.4 kJ/kg- solvent and 6.35 kJ/kg-CO2), exhibiting a significantly lower energy requirement for regeneration. The relative heat duty indicator clearly reconfirmed that the required heat for CO2 regeneration dropped markedly to 48.64% in GlyK-50 (51.36% energy reduction) and continued decreasing to 40.86% in GlyK-60 (59.14% energy reduction). Though many studies have confirmed that there is some discrepancy between the required heat duty of lab-scale investigations and largescale plants [36,39], the 59.1% energy reduction reflects that the aqueous-based phase change GlyK-60 solvent has a great potential to reduce the energy penalty of large-scale CO2 capture processes.

texts.orig: The decrease in the regeneration heat requirement of phase change GlyK-X solvents can be ascribed to two factors. First, only the CO2-rich phase as a portion of the whole solvent is regenerated and the upper liquid phase as the CO2-free (50-63 vol%) phase is directly recycled to the absorption column without any thermal regeneration which can significantly decrease energy consumption. Second, the accumulation of CO2-containing molecules in the CO2-rich phase can significantly increase the concentration of these species as one of the main driving forces for CO2 regeneration and facilitate the rate of CO2 desorption. To gain a better understanding, the concentration of released CO2 during the regeneration of aqueous-based phase change GlyK-60 solvent was compared to the aqueous GlyK-0 and MEA-30% solvents (Fig. 8c). The comparative results revealed that the maximum concentration of released CO2 during the regeneration of GlyK-60 (48.8 vol%) was 63.1%

texts.orig: higher than that of the aqueous GlyK-0 solvent (29.64 vol%) resulting in 50 min reduction in the regeneration time from 130 to 80 min. This reduction not only can save a huge amount of energy during regeneration, but also can decrease the residence time in the stripping column, equipment size and capital costs. Considering the same operating conditions during the regeneration process of all aqueous and aqueous-based phase change solvents, the mentioned difference in the CO2 desorption rate between GlyK-0 and GlyK-60 can be attributed to the extra-high concentration of CO2 molecules in the CO2-rich phase of GlyK-60. It should be pointed out that the regeneration temperature was set at the boiling point of solvent which was in the range of 100.5-101.1 °C for the aqueous GlyK-0 solvent and 105.5-108.22 °C for the phase change GlyK-60 solvent. The reason is probably due to the presence of DMF in the solution with a high boiling point (153 °C) which can significantly elevate the boiling point of mixture and accelerate the desorption rate of CO2 molecules. As Fig. 8c shows, aqueous GlyK and MEA solvents demonstrated very similar desorption profiles. However, due to the higher concentration of conventional MEA solvent (30 wt% or 5 M) than that of the GlyK solvent (3 M) and, subsequently, its higher CO2 absorption capacity, MEA-30% solvents released much more CO2 during 130 min of regeneration.

During the cyclic operation of phase change GlyK-60 solvent, the upper liquid phase was removed from the solvent before the regeneration process and added again to the solvent before the next cycle. As demonstrated in Fig. 9a, the absorption capacity of GlyK-60 solvent 16.5% (0.28 mol/L) decreased from 1.69 mol/L at the first to 1.41 mol/L at the second cycle. After the second cycle, no explicit change was observed and the absorption capacity remained nearly constant at 1.38 mol/L which certifies the stability of GlyK-60 solvent during cyclic operation. texts.orig: To further assess the cyclic performance of phase change GlyK-60 solvent, the CO2 concentration (Fig. 9b), temperature (Fig. 9c) and consumed energy (Fig. 9d) were dynamically monitored during the cyclic CO2 absorption-desorption experiments. It was found that due to the decreasing cyclic absorption capacity of the GlyK-60 solvent during successive cycles, the absorption time dropped from 145 to 115 min followed by a decline in both regeneration time and peak concentration from 91 to 75 min and 48.3 to 41.3 vol%, respectively. Also, the temperature of both the jacket and reactor showed nearly the same pattern at different cycles which resulted in the same amount of required energy at different cycles.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The mixture of GlyK/DMF/H2O was suggested as an aqueous-based phase change solvent with an excellent CO2 absorption capacity, high regeneration efficiency and comparable energy consumption. To study the role of DMF as an antisolvent in the aqueous-based solution, a series of amino acid salt GlyK-X solvents with different DMF:H2O volume ratios was prepared and tested. Among all GlyK-X solvents, the GlyK-60 solvent with DMF:H2O volume ratio of 60:40 reached the maximum volume of upper liquid phase (63%) with the CO2 absorption capacity of 0.546 mol/mol. The results of 1 H NMR and 13 C NMR indicated that the upper liquid phase of GlyK-X solvents contains no carbamate, bicarbonate and any other CO2-containing species. This promising feature of the aqueous-based GlyK-60 solvent as well as its high volume of the upper liquid phase led to a 11% increase of regeneration efficiency, 38.5% reduction of regeneration time and a 59.1% decrease of relative heat duty, compared to those of the conventional aqueous GlyK solvent. The energy required for the regeneration of aqueous-based phase change GlyK-60 was 483.3 kJ/kg-solvent or 6.32 MJ/kg-CO2 which was much less than the previously reported values. Due to the partial utilization of antisolvent (instead of using nonaqueous solvent) and very low energy consumption for the regeneration, the aqueous-based phase change GlyK-60 solvent can be used as a promising and cost-effective solvent for large-scale CO2 capture.
